chunk_summary: This section discusses functional dysphagia risk stratification using GMFCS and EDACS, emphasizing risk communication, classification levels, and a triage schema for feeding safety in neurodisability contexts.
learning_objectives: Understand the EDACS classification system and its levels for assessing eating and drinking safety and efficiency.
Learn how GMFCS levels correlate with dysphagia and aspiration risk in neurodisability.
Apply a triage schema to stratify dysphagia risk based on EDACS and GMFCS levels.
Recognize the role of Mini-EDACS in assessing feeding development in children aged 18-36 months.
key_facts: EDACS levels range from I (safe and efficient) to V (unsafe, tube feeding likely needed).
Mini-EDACS is used for children aged 18-36 months to assess early feeding skills.
High GMFCS levels (IV-V) are associated with increased dysphagia and aspiration risk due to factors like scoliosis, reflux, and ineffective cough.
A triage schema categorizes risk as low (EDACS I-II, GMFCS I-III, no recurrent infections), moderate (EDACS III or EDACS II with complications), or high (EDACS IV-V or severe feeding-related issues).
In India, delayed complementary feeding and caregiver fear of choking are common, making EDACS and its Hindi translation particularly relevant.
reference_quote: EDACS classifies usual eating and drinking performance (from age 3 years) by safety (choking and aspiration risk) and efficiency (time, effort, oral loss): level I is safe and efficient; level V cannot eat or drink safely and tube feeding may be needed.
document_key_topics: GMFCS and its relationship to dysphagia risk
Triage schema for functional dysphagia risk
